nativeRange.habitat: Hard substrates including reef and rocky surfaces in shallow tropical waters, typically in the photic zone

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that may graze on macroalgae (e.g. tangs, rabbitfish in smaller tanks)
urchins known to consume calcareous algae

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing for Coin Halimeda (Halimeda discoidea). Shows characteristic large rounded coin-shaped CaCO3 segments. Sole hobby-trade source for this specialty entry.
Secondary product image from Mosaic Macros listing.

summary: Coin Halimeda is a calcareous green macroalga with distinctively large, rounded, coin-shaped segments heavily impregnated with calcium carbonate. Valued as a display specimen in reef tanks, it grows slowly upward from live rock and is largely unpalatable to most reef fish.
careNotes:
Coin Halimeda (Halimeda discoidea) is a calcareous green macroalga distinguished from other Halimeda species by its notably large, flattened, rounded segments — sometimes described as coin- or disc-like — which are heavily impregnated with calcium carbonate. It grows upward from the substrate, anchoring via a short holdfast to hard surfaces such as live rock or rocky reef substrate, making it a natural fit as a display piece in the aquascape.

CALCIUM AND ALKALINITY: As a calcareous alga, H. discoidea deposits substantial calcium carbonate (aragonite, 60–80% of dry mass) into its segments. In a healthy, actively growing specimen, this calcification process measurably consumes calcium and alkalinity from the water column. Maintaining standard reef calcium levels (380–420 ppm Ca, 1,250–1,350 ppm Mg) and stable alkalinity (8–12 dKH) is generally advised. Drops in calcium or alkalinity availability can stall growth and cause segment whitening.

LIGHTING: High-intensity lighting is generally advised; calcareous Halimeda species have been documented to require ample photosynthetically active radiation to support both photosynthesis and active calcification. Medium-to-high PAR (150+ µmol/m²/s at the placement point) is commonly recommended by experienced hobbyists. Insufficient lighting is a primary cause of poor growth in captivity.

GROWTH RATE: Growth tends to be slow relative to non-calcareous macroalgae, reflecting the metabolic cost of depositing calcium carbonate in each new segment. This slow growth rate is normal and should not be misinterpreted as decline.

SHIPPING WHITENING: Coin Halimeda commonly arrives appearing white or pale following shipment, because chloroplasts migrate deeper into the tissue in darkness. This is normal behavior; coloration typically returns within hours of light exposure.

GRAZING RESISTANCE: The heavily calcified segments are generally unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates, making this species relatively grazing-resistant compared to non-calcareous macroalgae. However, urchins and some larger herbivorous fish have been reported to consume Halimeda segments in aquaria.

SIBLING SPECIES: See also cactus-halimeda (H. monile) for a cylindrical-segment Halimeda, and carpeting-halimeda (H. opuntia) for a mat-forming creeping form.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is typically achieved by fragmentation — breaking off a healthy segment chain and wedging it among rockwork or attaching it to a plug. Sexual reproduction, which in green algae typically involves an alternating cycle of spore-producing and gamete-producing generations, has not been documented in hobby aquaria. The genus Halimeda is documented to complete sexual reproduction rapidly (in approximately 36 hours) in nature, with individual thalli (the leafy body of the alga) living several months to roughly a year in wild populations; aquarium specimens may persist longer under stable conditions.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source for this specialty entry. Product listing for 'Coin Halimeda' (Halimeda discoidea). Provides identification, morphology description (heavily calcified, grows upward from substrate, unpalatable to most fish), product imagery, and shipping whitening note.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Halimeda discoidea
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmed: Family Halimedaceae, Order Bryopsidales, Class Ulvophyceae. Native range: widespread tropical oceans including Hawaiian Islands. Habitat: hard bottoms (reef and rocky surfaces) at depths ~0.9–30.5 m. Adult size up to 10 cm. Common name 'Money Plant' noted. Attaches to hard substrate via holdfast.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Halimeda (genus)
additional.notes: Genus-level calcium carbonate deposition data: segments 60–80% aragonite by dry mass. Calcium and magnesium requirements for calcareous Halimeda extrapolated from this source. Growth rate context: sexual reproduction in ~36 hours, thallus lifespan months to ~1 year in wild populations.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
